title: About
text: Hi! My name is Katie Freeman, and I’m a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln double majoring in advertising and public relations and sports media communication with a minor in english. I’ve been on the dean’s list every semester in college. Currently, I am a Runner/Agency Assistant at Swanson Russell. I assist with administrative tasks and day-to-day office operations. Previously, I was the marketing and special events intern at the Brain Injury Association of Nebraska, where I created advertising campaigns on Canva and Adobe Creative Cloud, managed and shared on social media accounts including Facebook and Instagram, and helped with the planning and execution of their annual conference. I also worked closely with the marketing coordinator to create advertising strategies to help spread brain injury awareness. I am a go-getter, self-starter, leader of group projects and a team player. This is my sports blog for SPMC 350: Sport Data Analysis and Visualization.

title: Is Pryce Sandfort the Most Effective High Volume Three-Point Shooter Among Big Ten Players?
text:
Examining the Three-Point Efficiency of the Husker Forward Among Others in the Big Ten.
When examining all Big Ten players who average at least 5 threes per game, we can filter the top players.
The ability to shoot efficiently from beyond the arc, while maintaining high volume, is one of the most valuable skills a college basketball player can bring to the court. This raises an important question for the 2025-2026 Big Ten season. Is Pryce Sandfort the most effective high-volume three-point shooter in the conference? To answer this question
Players who take a high volume of shots from above the arch tend to see their efficiency drop. As defenses focus in on them, attempts become more difficult and consistency becomes harder to maintain. Across college basketball, especially in conferences as competitive as the Big Ten, most players fall into one of two categories. High-volume shooters or efficient ones. But, Pryce Sandfort may just fall into both.
High-volume shooters are players attempting at least 5+ three-pointers per game. Their efficiency is measured by their 3-point percentage. A blend of total makes, attempts and accuracy helps separate players who can maintain that level of accuracy while taking a large number of attempts.
Before evaluating efficiency, it’s important to understand how much Sandfort is shooting. Volume can separate players and Sandfort sits at the top of the conference. Focusing on just Big Ten players who average at least 5 threes per game, we can see right where Sandfort is.
Plot #1 — delete


Code
library(tidyverse)

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

nu <- bigten |>
  filter(Team == "Nebraska")

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player", 
    y="3-Point Attempts", 
    title="No One Shoots More Than Sandfort",
    subtitle="Among players averaging at least 5 threes per game, he leads the Big Ten in three-point attempts", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Sandfort doesn’t just take a lot of threes, he takes more than anyone else in the Big Ten. However, this often comes with a downside. The more a player shoots, the more difficult those shots become, leading to lower percentages.
This raises another question of is Sandfort sacrificing efficiency for volume?
To answer this question, we need to look at how Sandfort compares to other high-volume shooters while comparing efficiency.
Plot #2 — delete


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data=bigten,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="grey",
    alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(
    data=nu5,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="#e41c38"
  ) +
  scale_size(range = c(1,10), name="3-Point Attempts") +
    geom_text_repel(
      data=bigten,
      aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, label=Player)
    ) +
labs(
    x="3-Point Field Goals", 
    y="3-Point Percentage", 
    title="High Volume Doesn't Mean Lower Efficiency for Sandfort", 
    subtitle="Most high-volume shooters see their accuracy drop, but Sandfort proves that doesn't have to happen.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot")









While many high-volume shooters drift toward a lower efficiency, Sandfort remains firmly in the 40% range. His position on the chart, high volume and strong in accuracy, puts him in a rare category in the conference.
This chart doesn’t just show how well he shoots, but how often, which few players can combine these two traits at this level.
It’s impressive to be efficient at a high volume, it’s even more impressive to rank among the best in both categories.


Code
bar1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Point Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bar2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Pointers Made"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

library(patchwork)

bar2 + bar1 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Sandfort Shoots More and Shoots Better Than Most",
    subtitle = "He leads the Big Ten in three-point attempts and sits among the conference's most accurate shooters.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Sandfort leads the conference in three-pointers made per game and doesn’t fall far behind in accuracy, ranking near the top of the Big Ten in three-point percentage.
This combination is what makes Sandfort a dangerous player.
You can’t afford to leave him open because of his efficiency. You also can’t shut him down, because his volume ensures he will continue to get opportunities. This dual threat forces opponnets into difficult decisions with every possession.
He isn’t just a high-volume player. He’s an efficient one, too. That’s what makes him arguably the most dangerous three-point shooter in the Big Ten.
The Pryce is right!
Plot #5 — delete


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(dplyr)

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")


Rows: 241 Columns: 31
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (5): Player, Team, Pos, Awards, Player-additional
dbl (26): Rk, G, GS, MP, FG, FGA, FG%, 3P, 3PA, 3P%, 2P, 2PA, 2P%, eFG%, FT,...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.


Code
shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

best <- shooters |>
  select(Player, Team, `3PA`, `3P`) |>
  arrange(desc(`3PA`))

best |> gt() |>
  cols_label(
    `3PA` = "3-Point Field Goal Attempts",
    `3P` = "3-Point Field Goals"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Three-Point Attempts vs. Three-Pointers Made",
    subtitle = "Sandfort at the top again for attemps and made"
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**Source:** Sports Reference | **By:** Katie Freeman")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#e41c38"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = Team == "Nebraska")
  )
